senses.definition: to become more powerful, intense, or larger in quantity

examples.x: As the tension builds up, everyone anticipates the final match.
Confidence tends to build up when you achieve small goals.
Pressure can build up in a closed system if not released properly.
The excitement is starting to build up as the concert date approaches.
Over time, clutter can build up in the attic if not addressed.

senses.definition: to increase something, such as a quantity, level, or value

examples.x: The manager's decision to bump up employee benefits was well-received.
The software update aims to bump up the efficiency of the application.
The sudden surge in demand forced the company to bump up manufacturing capacity.
The successful marketing campaign helped bump up the sales figures for the quarter.
The government plans to bump up funding for education in the next fiscal year.

senses.definition: to gradually add to the amount, number, price, etc. of something

examples.x: Prices at the gas pump started to creep up, causing concerns among commuters.
The number of COVID-19 cases began to creep up again, prompting authorities to implement safety measures.
The interest rates on loans may slowly creep up over time, impacting borrowers' monthly payments.
As the project progressed, the scope began to creep up, requiring additional resources and time.

senses.definition: to reduce pressure, intensity, or pace of something to make someone feel more at ease

examples.x: After weeks of intensive training, the coach decided to let the team ease up for a few days.
As the traffic congestion cleared, the commute started to ease up.
As the tension in the room dissipated, the atmosphere began to ease up.

senses.definition: to suddenly become more intense, especially in terms of a situation or conflict

examples.x: The argument between the neighbors flared up again, and the police were called.
Political tensions in the region continue to flare up, causing concerns for international peace.
The dispute over the inheritance flared up during the reading of the will.
The team's performance had been stable, but their issues with teamwork flared up during a crucial project.
The social media discussion quickly flared up into a heated debate over politics.

senses.definition: to increase in value, extent, amount, etc.

examples.x: The demand for the product caused its price to go up.
Real estate prices have been going up steadily in this area.
The company's profits are expected to go up significantly this year.
The temperature is forecasted to go up throughout the week.
Due to inflation, the cost of living has gone up.

senses.definition: to act more quickly because there is not much time

examples.x: He doesn’t need to hurry up as he’s already finished his work.
Hurry up, your friends are waiting for you outside.
I usually hurry up when I see the bus coming.
The teacher told the students to hurry up with their assignments.

senses.definition: to increase the price of something
to become less intense or strong

examples.x: The heavy rain let up after a few hours, giving way to sunny skies.
The intensity of the earthquake let up after the initial tremors, causing less damage than initially feared.
The protesters' chants let up as the police arrived, creating a more subdued atmosphere.
The demand for the new product let up after the initial hype, settling into a more steady pace.
The pain in my leg let up after I took the pain medication, allowing me to move more freely.

senses.definition: to experience a positive rise in something

examples.x: After a quiet winter, hotel bookings began to pick up in spring.
Business always picks up during the holiday season.
As word spread about the festival, ticket sales started to pick up.
After the announcement, the stock market picked up considerably.
Once the reviews came out, sales for the book really started to pick up.

senses.definition: to increase in amount or quantity over time

examples.x: If you don't address your issues, they'll just pile up over time.
Bills just seem to pile up when you least expect it.
The snow has been piling up all night; we'll need to shovel it in the morning.
Work assignments have been piling up since the manager went on vacation.
Leaves piled up in the backyard during autumn.

senses.definition: to increase or enhance something

examples.x: They decided to pump up the advertising budget to attract more customers.
To keep up with demand, the factory had to pump up production rates.
Due to the positive response from users, the app developers decided to pump up the features in the next update.

senses.definition: to cause an increase in the amount, number, or value of something

examples.x: High demand for the product pushed up its price.
The sudden boom in tourism pushed up the property values in the coastal town.

senses.definition: to increase the speed of an engine

examples.x: The mechanic revved up the engine to test its performance.
He revved up the motorcycle's engine before speeding off.
In a drag race, drivers rev up their engines to get a quick start.

senses.definition: to cause the cost or value of something to increase

examples.x: Global events can run up the price of commodities.
A surge in demand can run up the prices of luxury goods.
Innovations in technology often run up the value of related stocks.
The celebrity's endorsement ran up the sales of the cosmetic brand.
The limited edition nature of the item ran up its market value.

senses.definition: to cause the value or price of something to rise

examples.x: The technological advancements in smartphones have sent up their prices in recent years.
The positive reviews of the restaurant's new menu sent up its popularity among diners.
The company's successful marketing campaign sent up the sales of their new product.
The positive reviews of the restaurant's new menu sent up its popularity among diners.

senses.definition: (of an amount or price) to increase rapidly

examples.x: After the release of their new product, the company's stock prices shot up.
The market volatility caused the commodity prices to shoot up.
The sudden shortage of supplies caused prices to shoot up.
During the holiday season, sales in retail stores typically shoot up.
His blood pressure shot up when he heard the shocking news.
The unexpected event caused expenses to shoot up for the project.

senses.definition: to decrease in speed or pace

examples.x: The production line started to slow up due to technical issues with the machinery.
As the weather worsened, the runners began to slow up, finding it challenging to maintain their pace.
As the sun set, the construction work on the highway naturally began to slow up.
The river current began to slow up as it entered the wider, calmer section.

senses.definition: to become faster

examples.x: The river currents began to speed up after heavy rainfall in the upstream areas.
As the storm approached, the winds began to speed up, causing trees to sway vigorously.
In the second half of the game, the pace started to speed up as both teams aimed for more goals.
The wind started to speed up, making the sailing experience more exhilarating.
The heartbeat monitor indicated that the patient's heart rate began to speed up, requiring medical attention.

senses.definition: to increase the size, amount, intensity, speed, etc. of something

examples.x: The government decided to step up security measures in response to the increased threat.
The company management decided to step up their efforts to reduce carbon emissions.
The teacher asked the students to step up their participation in classroom discussions.
The supervisor asked the employee to step up their productivity to meet targets.

senses.definition: to turn a switch on a device so that it makes more sound, heat, etc.

examples.x: Can you turn up the radio?
I couldn't hear the music, so I turned up the volume on my headphones.
In the cold months, we often have to turn up the thermostat.
It's getting chilly in the room, let's turn up the heating.
The soup wasn't heating up fast enough, so she turned up the stove.

senses.definition: to cause an increase in the amount, size, or significance of something

examples.x: The government announced initiatives to scale up renewable energy sources to reduce carbon emissions.
As the population grows, cities need to scale up their public transportation systems to accommodate more commuters.
Investors can purchase a small stake in a promising startup and then scale up their investment as the company proves its viability and potential for growth.
To keep up with the competition, the restaurant decided to scale up its menu offerings and renovate its dining space.
